title: Marmion
content:
آ آ آ آ Heap on more wood! the wind is chill;
آ آ آ آ But let it whistle as it will,
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ll keep our Christmas merry still.
آ آ آ آ Each age has deemï؟½d the new-born year
آ آ آ آ The fittest time for festal cheer:
آ آ آ آ Even, heathen yet, the savage Dane
آ آ آ آ At Iol more deep the mead did drain;
آ آ آ آ High on the beach his galleys drew,
آ آ آ آ And feasted all his pirate crew;
آ آ آ آ Then in his low and pine-built hall
آ آ آ آ Where shields and axes deckï؟½d the wall
آ آ آ آ They gorged upon the half-dressï؟½d steer;
آ آ آ آ Caroused in seas of sable beer;
آ آ آ آ While round, in brutal jest, were thrown
آ آ آ آ The half-gnawï؟½d rib, and marrow-bone:
آ آ آ آ Or listenï؟½d all, in grim delight,
آ آ آ آ While Scalds yellï؟½d out the joys of fight.
آ آ آ آ Then forth, in frenzy, would they hie,
آ آ آ آ While wildly loose their red locks fly,
آ آ آ آ And dancing round the blazing pile,
آ آ آ آ They make such barbarous mirth the while,
آ آ آ آ As best might to the mind recall
آ آ آ آ The boisterous joys of Odinï؟½s hall.

آ آ آ آ And well our Christian sires of old
آ آ آ آ Loved when the year its course had rollï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And brought blithe Christmas back again,
آ آ آ آ With all his hospitable train.
آ آ آ آ Domestic and religious rite
آ آ آ آ Gave honour to the holy night;
آ آ آ آ On Christmas Eve the bells were rung;
آ آ آ آ On Christmas Eve the mass was sung:
آ آ آ آ That only night in all the year,
آ آ آ آ Saw the stoled priest the chalice rear.
آ آ آ آ The damsel donnï؟½d her kirtle sheen;
آ آ آ آ The hall was dressï؟½d with holly green;
آ آ آ آ Forth to the wood did merry-men go,
آ آ آ آ To gather in the mistletoe.
آ آ آ آ Then openï؟½d wide the Baronï؟½s hall
آ آ آ آ To vassal, tenant, serf and all;
آ آ آ آ Power laid his rod of rule aside
آ آ آ آ And Ceremony doffï؟½d his pride.
آ آ آ آ The heir, with roses in his shoes,
آ آ آ آ That night might village partner choose;
آ آ آ آ The Lord, underogating, share
آ آ آ آ The vulgar game of ï؟½post and pairï؟½.
آ آ آ آ All hailï؟½d, with uncontrollï؟½d delight,
آ آ آ آ And general voice, the happy night,
آ آ آ آ That to the cottage, as the crown,
آ آ آ آ Brought tidings of salvation down.

آ آ آ آ The fire, with well-dried logs supplied,
آ آ آ آ Went roaring up the chimney wide;
آ آ آ آ The huge hall-tableï؟½s oaken face,
آ آ آ آ Scrubbï؟½d till it shone, the day to grace,
آ آ آ آ Bore then upon its massive board
آ آ آ آ No mark to part the squire and lord.
آ آ آ آ Then was brought in the lusty brawn,
آ آ آ آ By old blue-coated serving-man;
آ آ آ آ Then the grim boarï؟½s head frownï؟½d on high,
آ آ آ آ Crested with bays and rosemary.
آ آ آ آ Well can the green-garbï؟½d ranger tell,
آ آ آ آ How, when, and where, the monster fell;
آ آ آ آ What dogs before his death to tore,
آ آ آ آ And all the baiting of the boar.
آ آ آ آ The wassel round, in good brown bowls,
آ آ آ آ Garnishï؟½d with ribbons, blithely trowls.
آ آ آ آ There the huge sirloin reekï؟½d; hard by
آ آ آ آ Plum-porridge stood, and Christmas pie;
آ آ آ آ Nor failï؟½d old Scotland to produce,
آ آ آ آ At such high tide, her savoury goose.
آ آ آ آ Then came the merry makers in,
آ آ آ آ And carols roarï؟½d with blithesome din;
آ آ آ آ If unmelodious was the song,
آ آ آ آ It was a hearty note, and strong.
آ آ آ آ Who lists may in their mumming see
آ آ آ آ Traces of ancient mystery;
آ آ آ آ White shirts supplied the masquerade,
آ آ آ آ And smutted cheeks the visors made;
آ آ آ آ But, O! what maskers, richly dight,
آ آ آ آ Can boast of bosoms half so light!
آ آ آ آ England was merry England, when
آ آ آ آ Old Christmas brought his sports again.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas Christmas broachï؟½d the mightiest ale;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas Christmas told the merriest tale;
آ آ آ آ A Christmas gambol oft could cheer
آ آ آ آ The poor manï؟½s heart through half the year.
meaning:
About: Sir Walter Scott, 1st Baronet was a prolific Scottish historical novelist and poet popular throughout Europe during his time.

In some ways Scott was the first author to have a truly international career in his lifetime, with many contemporary readers all over Europe, Australia, and North America. His novels and poetry are still read, and many of his works remain classics of both English-language literature and specifically, of Scottish literature. Famous titles include Ivanhoe, Rob Roy, The Lady of the Lake, Waverley and The Heart of Midlothian.
